1: Bemidbar Sinai = ‘in the wilderness of Sinai’ — the geographical anchor for the Second Passover law. The date formula ‘second year, first month’ is one of the few precise calendar datings in Numbers, and it places this event a month before the census of Numbers 1 (second month) — the book is not in strict chronological order.
2: Ha-pesach (ha-pesach, H6453): Passover, from pasach (H6452) = to pass over/skip; YHWH ‘passed over’ Israelite houses with blood on the doorposts (Ex Numbers 12:13). Mo'ado = ‘its appointed time’ (mo'ed, H4150), from ya'ad (H3259) = to appoint/meet. YHWH’s feast calendar is structured around mo'edim (appointed times), making the liturgical calendar an expression of covenant relationship.
3: Bein ha-arbayim = ‘between the evenings’ = at twilight/dusk; the ambiguous phrase (between sunset and full dark) was debated in later interpretation. Ke-khol-chuqqotav uke-khol-mishpatav = ‘according to all its statutes and all its rules’ (chuqqah, H2708 + mishpat, H4941): statute plus judicial ordinance — two complementary legal categories covering divine law comprehensively.
4: Moses commands the Passover and the Israelites comply. The pattern of divine command → Mosaic transmission → Israelite compliance is the literary backbone of Numbers. It creates a rhythm of speech-and-obedience that models the covenant relationship.
5: The Passover is kept on the fourteenth day, bein ha-arbayim (Numbers 9:3). The brevity of the compliance report (‘they did so’) against the extended law-giving creates a narrative of swift, complete obedience — ideal Israel executing YHWH’s command without deliberation.
6: Temei'im le-nefesh adam = ‘impure from a human corpse.’ Tameh (H2931) = impure; nefesh adam (H5315 + H120) = human soul/person; contact with the dead (met, H4191) created the most serious and long-lasting impurity (7-day contamination, Num 19). These men cannot observe the Passover on the appointed day.
7: The men ask: lammah niggara = ‘why should we be diminished/cut off?’ (gara', H1639, Niphal = to be diminished, cut off from). The verb conveys exclusion from the community’s covenant act. Their concern is not technical compliance but genuine inclusion in Israel’s redemptive identity.
8: Imdu va-eshme'ah = ‘stand, and I will hear what YHWH commands about you.’ Moses’s response models prophetic judicial consultation. Shama' (H8085) = to hear/heed; the same root gives the Shema (‘Hear, O Israel’). Hearing YHWH’s word is the primary posture of the covenant mediator.
9: YHWH speaks directly with the ruling. The pattern of case-by-case divine legal consultation (Numbers 9:8; Numbers 15:34; Numbers 27:5) establishes that biblical law was not a closed, static corpus but developed through real pastoral situations brought before YHWH through Moses.
10: Le-nefesh tameh = impure from a dead person. Uvo-derekh rechokah = ‘on a distant road.’ Rechokah (H7350) = distant, far. The two qualifying conditions — death-impurity and physical distance — are the pastoral realities that the Second Passover provision addresses. Both represent involuntary exclusion from the appointed time.
11: Ba-chodesh ha-sheni be-arba'ah asar yom = ‘in the second month, on the fourteenth day.’ Exactly one month after the regular Passover. Al-matzot umerorim yokhluhu = ‘they shall eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.’ Matzot (H4682) = unleavened bread (haste, affliction); merorim (H4844) = bitter herbs (the bitterness of slavery). The delayed Passover retains all the original elements.
12: Etzem lo yishberu vo = ‘a bone they shall not break in it.’ Etzem (H6106) = bone; the structural integrity of the lamb is maintained. The Passover-bone law (also Ex Numbers 12:46) is cited explicitly as fulfilled in Jesus in John 19:36. Ke-khol-chuqqat ha-Pesach ya'asu oto = according to all the statute (chuqqat, H2708) of the Passover.
13: The clean person who voluntarily omits the Passover is cut off: nikkrata (from karat, H3772, Niphal = to be cut off). Karet is the most severe communal penalty: exclusion from the covenant community. The absence of legitimate exemption (no impurity, no distant journey) makes willful non-observance inexcusable and carries the full consequence.
14: Ki ger yagur itkhem = ‘and if a sojourner sojourns among you.’ Ger (H1616) = resident alien; gur (H1481) = to dwell as a stranger. The cognate absolute (ger yagur) is emphatic. Chuqqah achat = ‘one statute’ — the universalizing principle: the same law for all residents. This appears throughout the Holiness Code (Leviticus 24:22) and foreshadows the gospel’s removal of ethnic barriers.
15: Ve-yom haqim et-ha-mishkan = ‘and on the day the tabernacle was erected.’ Mishkan (H4908) = dwelling/tabernacle, from shakan (H7931) = to dwell, settle. YHWH’s shekhinah (dwelling-presence) is linguistically embedded in the tabernacle’s name. Yechasseh ha-anan = ‘the cloud covered’ (kisah, H3680, Piel); the cloud both reveals and veils YHWH’s presence.
16: Ke-en ha-esh = ‘like the appearance of fire.’ Mar'eh (H4758) = appearance/vision; esh (H784) = fire. The night-fire and day-cloud are matched to human perception: visible phenomena indicating YHWH’s presence without revealing his person. The dual appearance serves visibility across the full 24-hour cycle.
17: Uve-he'alot ha-anan = ‘and when the cloud was taken up’ (alah, H5927, Niphal = to be lifted; same root as behaalotekha, Numbers 8:2). Israel travels at the cloud’s rising and camps at its settling. Nasa (H5265) = to set out/break camp; chanah (H2583) = to encamp. YHWH’s movement defines the community’s movement.
18: Al-pi YHWH = ‘at the mouth of YHWH’ — by YHWH’s command. Peh (H6310) = mouth; the mouth of YHWH = his word/command. This phrase recurs 9 times in Numbers 9:18-23, hammering the point: Israel moves and stops only at YHWH’s word. The cloud is the visible form of the divine word.
19: Mishmart YHWH = ‘the charge/guard of YHWH’ (mishmeret, H4931 = charge, from shamar, H8104 = to keep, guard). When the cloud rests long on the tabernacle, Israel waits. The extended cloud-dwelling tests patience: can Israel wait on YHWH without forcing movement by their own schedule?
20: The rhetorical pattern of Numbers 9:20-23 lists multiple temporal scenarios (two days, a month, a year) to make the point exhaustive: regardless of duration, Israel follows the cloud. Beyamim mispar = ‘for a number of days’; chodesh yamim = a month of days; yamim rabbim = many days — all governed by the same al-pi YHWH principle.
21: Im-hayah ha-anan me-erev ad-boker = ‘if the cloud was from evening to morning.’ Even a nighttime lifting triggers departure at dawn. YHWH’s timing does not conform to Israel’s preferred schedule. Uvyom uvalayla = ‘whether by day or by night’ — the cloud’s signal is authoritative at any hour.
22: Shenayim yamim o chodesh o yamim = ‘two days, or a month, or longer.’ The escalating temporal series reaches an open-ended ‘yamim’ (extended time). Yishkon = ‘it dwells’ (shakan, H7931); the same root as mishkan. When the cloud-dwelling extends, Israel’s camping extends correspondingly.
23: Al-pi YHWH yachanu ve-al-pi YHWH yissa'u = ‘at the mouth of YHWH they camped and at the mouth of YHWH they set out.’ The chapter’s summary verse: camp/mouth/YHWH // mouth/YHWH/set-out. Mishmart YHWH shamaru = ‘the charge of YHWH they kept’ (cognate absolute: mishmeret + shamar). Israel’s wilderness faithfulness is encapsulated here.
